topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus map, usually a comforting landscape of familiar pathways, felt suddenly stark and final in my hands. I traced the outline of the main quad with a fingertip, a faint tremor running through my arm. "This can't be right," I muttered, the sound thin in the cavernous library. My backpack, still stuffed with textbooks and half-finished sketches, felt like a heavy anchor dragging me down. I bounced my knee rapidly against the worn wooden table, a restless energy thrumming just beneath my skin. I needed to find the administration office, needed to demand an explanation, to find a loophole. The thought of this place vanishing, of all the opportunities I’d poured into it dissolving into dust, made my chest tighten with a frantic need to act, to salvage something.

[story 2]

"So, it's actually happening?" I asked, my voice pitched higher than I intended, leaning forward across the cramped coffee shop table. I didn't wait for the answer; I was already formulating a plan. My fingers drummed a quick, impatient rhythm on the laminated menu. I glanced at my watch, then back at the notice tacked haphazardly to the bulletin board—the final liquidation announcement. A sharp, almost painful anticipation coiled in my stomach. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor echoing loudly in the sudden silence of my focus. I had to talk to Sarah, to the student council president, and I had to figure out how to make this whole thing stop before the final deadline.

[story 3]

I paced the length of my dorm room three times, my worn sneakers squeaking on the linoleum. The official notice lay crumpled on my desk, the ink seeming to mock my stillness. A spark ignited behind my eyes—a hot, insistent feeling that demanded movement, demand for change. I snatched my phone, my thumb flying across the screen as I started calling contacts, my breathing growing shallow and quick. "Just tell me what to do," I whispered into the receiver, my tone sharp, brimming with an uncontainable drive. The closing of the college felt less like a loss and more like a starting gun. I needed to be the first one moving, the first one fighting for a future that still felt entirely open.
